text: Immediate sternotomy is performed and the anatomy is inspected.
Here you can compare them to the preoperative cardiac MRI images.
All the major vessels are then circumferentially mobilized with controlling the aorta, main pulmonary artery, and vena cava.
The vent site is then dissected out.
The patient is then aorto-bicavally cannulated and cardiopulmonary bypass is initiated.
A vent is placed through the right upper pulmonary vein and CO2 is brought onto the field.
The enlarged aortic root is then dissected down to the proximal coronary arteries.
The aortic cross clamp is then applied and there's a transverse aortotomy above the sinotubular junction.
In this patient, cardioplegia was delivered directly to the coronaries due to the level of aortic insufficiency, which by cardiac MRI was measured as moderate.
Once cardioplegic arrest has been reached, the aortic valve pathology is inspected.

This patient had a functionally bicuspid aortic valve with fusion of the left and right leaflets shown here on the preoperative transesophageal echocardiogram, resulting in severe stenosis and again, moderate insufficiency.
The pulmonary autograft is then harvested.
First, the distal pulmonary artery is transected proximal to the bifurcation and the pulmonary valve is thoroughly inspected.
This patient was found to be trileaflet with thin mobile leaflets.
Once the autograft is found to be suitable, traction sutures are placed in the aortic valve commissures.
The coronary artery buttons are identified and excised.
Once the left coronary artery button has been excised, the proximal coronary artery is then mobilized off the heart to the circumflex and left anterior descending coronary artery bifurcation.

Once the left coronary artery button has been excised and proximal coronary artery has been mobilized, the right coronary artery button is then excised and the proximal coronary artery button is mobilized.
Retraction sutures are placed in the top of both coronary artery buttons and the aortic valve is excised with 11 blade Metzenbaum scissors and electrocautery.
The site is marked on the right ventricular outflow tract through the main pulmonary artery with a right angle clamp.
The pulmonary autograft is then harvested.
Anterior surface of the proximal autograft is incised two to three centimeters below the nadir of the pulmonary valve.
The incision is extended, ensuring adequate circumferential flap of infundibular muscle.
Enucleation of the pulmonary autograft is then completed by separating the autograft posteriorly from the left ventricle.
Care is taken to avoid the first cephaloperforator branch of the left anterior descending coronary artery.

This branch of the coronary artery typically originates behind the medial posterior commissure of the pulmonary valve and courses into the infundibular septum towards the anterior papillary muscle of the tricuspid valve.
Both the aortic root and pulmonary autograft are sized.
The proximal autograft is then secured to the sinus of L-salva graft.
The autograft commissures are aligned with the commissure lines on the sinus of L-salva graft and secured with a 5-0 proline suture.
Each end of those proline sutures are then run continuously towards a midpoint between adjacent commissures and tied.
The distal body of the sinus valesalva graft is then telescoped over the skirt.
The distal autograft is secured to the sinus valesalva graft in an identical fashion.
The autograft commissures are aligned with the commissure markers on the graft and 4L proline is run towards the adjacent commissure and tied.
The graft is then untelescoped and examined and the valve is tested by floating it with saline and pressurized.
Autograft here is well seated within the sinus of El Salvo graft and the autograft leaflets co-apt well within the graft.
The now supported autograft is placed into the left ventricular outflow tract for securement.
The nadir of the autograft sinus is aligned to match the left coronary sinus of El Salvo.
Proximal anastomosis is initiated just below the nadir of the left coronary sinus.
The conal aortoventricularplasty is then performed due to the mildly hypoplastic aortic annulus.
An approximately one centimeter incision is made of the right left commissure into the interventricular septum.
The anastomosis is then completed anteriorly incorporating the area of left ventricular outflow tract enlargement that did not require a patch.
The interior suture line of the proximal autograft is then reinforced with a row of 4-row pledges supported interrupted mattress sutures.

A defect is then created in the left sinus of valsalva in the autograft with an 11 blade and high temperature ophthalmic cautery is then used to create a defect in the corresponding area in the sinus of valsalva graft.
The left coronary artery button is then re-implanted in the autograft, incorporating all three layers into the anastomosis and is carried out with a continuous 5-0 proline suture.
A 30 millimeter pulmonary homograft is then prepared.
The graft is trimmed at the branch pulmonary arteries.
The distal anastomosis is then completed with a continuous 5-0 proline suture.
The dilated section of the ascending aorta is then resected.
The commisural posts are resected and the distal supported autograft is trimmed.
The distal supported autograft is then anastomosed to the ascending aorta with a continuous 4-0 proline suture and a Teflon felt strip buttress.
An anti-grade cardioplegia line is placed in the neo-aortic root.
The supported autograft is then pressurized.
This allows right ventricular outflow tract bleeders to be identified and hemostasis to be achieved with electrocautery and pleasure supported sutures.
Pressurization of the root also allows for a more accurate site to be identified for right coronary artery button.
It usually fits best in the non-coronary artery sinus.
A defect is then created in the sported autograft and the right coronary artery button is reimplanted in identical fashion to the left coronary artery button.
Following implantation, any residual adventitial attachments to the coronary artery are removed and the proximal supported autograft suture line is secured further with Prevalic adhesive.
Finally, the right ventricular outflow tract is then reconstructed by the proximal pulmonary homograph being anastomosed to the right ventricular outflow tract with a continuous 5-0 proline suture.
The heart is de-aired, the aortic cross clamp is removed, and the patient was successfully weaned and separated from cardiopulmonary bypass.
Using an ophthalmic cautery, a small defect is created in the right sinus of Valsalva in the autograft.
This defect prevents blood reaccumulation between the autograft and the sinus of Valsalva graft.
Thank you very much for watching.
